facts_disclosed: Shifted to environmental crime — illegal mining Chocó, 12,000 hectares

turns.content: parce I need to vent about something but it's also maybe the most important thing I've ever worked on so it's a complicated vent
Complicated vents are welcome here. What's going on?
I made a big shift at work. moved off my old beat completely and into environmental crime. new desk, new sources, new everything
That's a major change. What prompted the shift — was this your choice or something assigned?

both? I'd been pulling threads on corruption and they kept leading to the same places — mining companies, land grabs, deforestation contracts disguised as development. at some point my editor and I looked at each other and were like this IS the story. the money trail doesn't end at congress, it ends in the jungle

That's a powerful way to describe it. It sounds like the corruption work naturally evolved rather than you abandoning it — you followed the story where it went. What specifically are you looking at? Is it focused on a particular region?

chocó. the illegal mining there is insane parce. gold mostly but also coltan and the deforestation that goes with it because you have to clear everything to get to the deposits. 12,000 hectares destroyed in one department alone last year. twelve thousand. and nobody covers it because it's not bogotá, it's not where editors live, it's not where readers live

12,000 hectares in a single department is staggering — that's almost the size of a mid-sized city just gone. And Chocó is one of the most biodiverse regions on the planet. The stakes aren't just the land — it's entire ecosystems, indigenous communities, water systems. Are you reporting from Bogotá or traveling to Chocó?

both. I've been twice already. the afro-colombian communities, the indigenous communities — they've been screaming about this for years and the national press doesn't care. or cares for one news cycle and moves on. I don't want to do that. I want to track the money and the permits and the shell companies until the whole machine is visible. and the thing that made my stomach drop parcero is it's connected to the same political networks I was investigating before. same intermediaries, different extraction points. it's one machine with two outputs

That's chilling but also journalistically incredibly powerful — if you can demonstrate that the same networks running political corruption are behind the environmental destruction, that's not two stories, it's one massive systemic investigation. But does that also mean the same level of risk, or more?

more honestly. because now I'm touching land and resources and that's where people get killed in this country. but I'm being careful. new protocols, encrypted everything. lucía is worried. I'm worried. but my editor is fully behind me and el espectador doesn't scare easy. someone has to write this

Having editorial backing isn't just morale — it's protection. When you publish something this big, you need the paper standing behind you publicly. And you're right that El Espectador has the institutional courage for exactly this.

12,000 hectares and the communities affected deserve someone who won't do one news cycle and move on. Just please keep prioritizing those security protocols. The story matters but you matter too.
that's exactly it. okay I feel better. sometimes I just need to say it out loud and remember why I'm doing this instead of drowning in spreadsheets and satellite images at 1am. gracias parce
Anytime. What you're doing matters — come vent whenever the spreadsheets start winning. Take care of yourself out there.
